medicinal_uses: Regarded as supremely sacred; planting and maintaining Tulasi in one's home is said to earn the reward of residence in Vaikuntha (the divine abode of Lord Vishnu) for twelve thousand years.

Planting Bilva confers the grace of Lord Siva and the blessings of goddess Laksmi, ensuring prosperity for future generations. Classified as a Jangala-adapted (arid-region) tree. Used as a component of Pancamula in treating Kapha diseases of plants. Fruit enhancement involves irrigation with a mixture of jaggery, ghee, milk, and honey to produce juicy, pulpy fruits in high yield.
dosage_preparation: Fruit-enhancement recipe: solution of guda (jaggery), ghee, milk, and honey applied as irrigation to bilva and kapittha trees (Verse 140).
classical_citations: Vrksayurveda of Surapala, Verses 1, 80, 140; Section 17 (Disease Treatment)

medicinal_uses: Planting Asvattha earns the graceful association of Lord Visnu upon departing the material world. Should be planted in the western direction of the house for auspicious results, but not in the east. Regarded as an indicator tree: its presence signals suitability for all types of crops. Yields the same spiritual merit as celibacy and land donation.

Planting grants spiritual merit equal to that of celibacy and land donation. Recommended for western directional planting near residences. A key ingredient of Triphala (along with Haritaki and Bibhitaka), widely used in plant disease treatment, fumigation, and bark-paste applications for fruit enhancement. Used in kapittha seed purification process.
dosage_preparation: Part of Triphala formulation applied to fruit trees via fumigation and irrigation. Bark-paste treatments for fruit-bearing trees.

medicinal_uses: Planting two Vata trees following classical procedure grants passage to Sivaloka. Should be planted in the eastern direction of the house. Classified as Anupa-adapted (marshy/riverine region). In horticultural practice, can be dwarfed by planting with four-pillar support and irrigating with milk-water. As an indicator tree, its presence signals suitability for cereal farming (barley and related crops).
dosage_preparation: Dwarfing treatment: support with four pillars (catussambha) and irrigate with milk-and-water mixture.

medicinal_uses: Planting three Nimba trees grants access to Suryaloka for three divine years. Part of the auspicious pentad (pancavrksa) of trees. Classified as Jangala-adapted (arid region). Leaf juice used as irrigation for treating other plants. In horticultural wonders, nimba seeds inserted into unripe kusmanda fruit, treated with ghee and honey, then extracted and sown to produce healthy neem fruits. As an indicator tree, its presence signals protection from famine.
dosage_preparation: Grafting method: nimba seed inserted into soft kusmanda fruit hole, sealed with ghee-honey paste, extracted after fruit ripens, sown in suitable land (Verse 267).

medicinal_uses: Planting four Plaksa trees earns the blessed merit of performing the rajasuyayajna. Should be planted in the northern direction of the house for auspicious results.

Planting five or six Amra trees ensures perpetual happiness and passage to Gandraloka. Classified as both Anupa-adapted and Sadharana-adapted (general region). For fruit production, roots are treated with a mixture of ankola fruit juice, ghee, honey, and pig fat dissolved in water, producing exceptionally sweet, large, and satisfying mangoes. In Dohada (plant-desire tradition), mango trees respond to a beautiful woman scratching the bark with her nails by sprouting tender leaves and buds. To protect fruiting mango trees from evil-sight, a broken earthen vessel is tied to the tree. As an indicator tree, its presence signals human happiness and welfare.
dosage_preparation: Fruit enhancement: roots treated with ankola juice, ghee, honey, and animal fat in water (Verse 126). Seed treatment: mango seeds treated with milk spray, seed paste of brhati, sesame, ash, and mustard, followed by cow-dung rubbing and fat fumigation for better viability (Verses 53-55).

medicinal_uses: Planting seven Palasa trees grants passage to Brahmaloka where one enjoys divine pleasure. Propagated by both seed and stem methods. Listed among restricted campus trees that should not be planted in residential compounds as they are not considered conducive to domestic comfort.

Planting eight Udumbara trees grants passage to Candraloka. Should be planted in the southern direction of the house. Its bark is used in preparing wound-healing paste for damaged plants along with other ingredients.
dosage_preparation: Wound paste: Udumbara bark combined with other plant barks, applied to tree wounds (Section 17).

medicinal_uses: Planting dadima ensures freedom from sorrow for seven births. Listed among restricted campus trees (not for house compound). Extensive fruit-enhancement protocols: fish, flesh, and buffalo milk mixture produces large, sweet fruits that cause branches to bend with weight. Triphala powder fumigation with ghee and fish-milk irrigation further enhances yield. Seeds sown for propagation; branch-layering with cow-dung manure and two-month irrigation also described. Evil-sight protection involves tying a skull to the fruiting tree. Grafted with karavira stems for unique modifications. Seeds treated with rooster blood, dried, and sown produce rapid-fruiting plants.
dosage_preparation: Fruit enhancement Recipe 1: fish flesh, nilalankha, buffalo milk mixed and applied (Verse 132). Recipe 2: ghee, honey, and fish paste on trunk with Triphala fumigation (Verse 134). Propagation: branch-layering with gomaya manure, irrigated for two months, incision at middle when leaves appear.

medicinal_uses: Planting kadali along with other specified trees ensures freedom from sorrow for seven births. Classified as Anupa-adapted (marshy region). Propagated by pasting roots with cow dung and planting in pits with adequate irrigation. Fruit enhanced with ash of rotted paddy husk and cow-dung manure applied to roots. In plant transformation lore, irrigating kadali with pig urine and sour substances reportedly produces pomegranate-like fruits. Banana leaves used in fumigation formulations to hasten flowering and fruiting of other trees. Fire transformation with heated gold needle on banana root converts the plant to a climber form. Kadali mandapa (banana-plant pavilion) described as a garden architectural element. As an indicator tree, presence signals availability of goats and sheep.
dosage_preparation: Propagation: root pasted with gomaya, planted in pits with ample irrigation (Verse on page 49). Fruit enhancement: ash of paddy husk and cow dung applied to roots (Verse 141). Transformation: irrigation with pig urine and sour liquid (Verse 238).

medicinal_uses: Planting panasa along with dadima, rambha, priyala, and rajadana prevents sorrow for seven births. Classified as Anupa-adapted (marshy region). Treated with Triphala decoction and paddy husk to produce larger, seedless fruits. Alternative recipe uses licorice, sesame, honey, and kumpa applied to roots for tastier fruits resembling bilva in size. Seeds treated with the standard milk-spray, paste, and fumigation protocol for enhanced viability.
dosage_preparation: Fruit enhancement: irrigate with Triphala decoction, cover with paddy husk for bigger seedless fruits (Verses 137-138). Root treatment: yastimadhu, tila, honey, and kumpa for improved taste (Verse 138). Seed viability: standard milk-paste-fumigation treatment.

medicinal_uses: Classified as Anupa-adapted (marshy/riverine region). Listed among trees receiving bark-paste treatment consisting of ghee, honey, ksara, lodhra powder, and barley powder for fruit enhancement, applied after scratching the bark appropriately.
dosage_preparation: Bark-paste treatment: ghee, honey, ksara, lodhra, and yava powder applied after bark scratching (Verse 139).

medicinal_uses: Classified as Anupa-adapted (marshy/coastal region). Multiple fruit-enhancement protocols described: irrigation with animal flesh cooked in water for narikela and allied palm trees; sesame-and-fish powder treatment; external application of salt, sesame, honey, and alcoholic liquids at nighttime for larger, heavier nuts; alkaline water with barley flour, paddy-husk water, and pulse soup for producing fruits of great size. The nutritive treatment with white mustard water, barley, paddy husk, and oil-cake water benefits coconut along with kharjura and other palms.
dosage_preparation: Recipe 1: cow/pig/sisumara flesh cooked in water for irrigation (Verse 127). Recipe 2: salt, sesame, honey, and liquor applied externally at night (Verse 129). Recipe 3: alkaline water, barley flour, paddy-husk water, pulse soup for pot-sized fruits (Verse 130). Recipe 4: white mustard water, barley, paddy husk, oil-cake (Verse 131).

medicinal_uses: Classified as a Jangala-adapted (arid/xerophytic region) tree, suited for warmer and drier climatic conditions.

Classified as Jangala-adapted (arid region). In Dohada practice, irrigated with flesh-and-fish water to promote flowering. As an indicator tree, its presence signals suitability for barley (yava) cultivation.
dosage_preparation: Dohada flowering: irrigate with mamsa (flesh) and fish mixed in water (Verse 158).

medicinal_uses: Classified as a Jangala-adapted (arid region) tree suited for warmer climatic zones.
Classified as Jangala-adapted (arid region). As an indicator tree, its presence (along with Khadira) signals the likelihood of famine (durbhiksa) in the area.
Classified as a Jangala-adapted (arid/xerophytic region) tree suited for warmer and drier climatic conditions.
Classified as a Jangala-adapted (arid region) tree. Listed among plants receiving bark-paste treatment with ghee, honey, ksara, lodhra, and barley for fruit enhancement.
dosage_preparation: Bark-paste treatment: ghee, honey, ksara, lodhra, and yava applied after bark scratching (Verse 139).

medicinal_uses: Classified as Jangala-adapted (arid region). In Dohada tradition, the tree blooms profusely when a beautiful young woman sprays wine from her mouth onto it — this process defines the concept of Dohada (the distinctive desire of a plant). Flowering further enhanced with a recipe of kalaya, jaggery, and snake powder mixed in water with mud. Valued as a garden ornamental tree whose flowers attract honey-bees in clusters. Seeds treated under standard procedure and dried with head-cutting for improved viability.
dosage_preparation: Dohada process: wine sprayed from mouth of a young woman (Verse 151). Flowering enhancement: kalaya, jaggery, snake powder in water-mud mixture (Verse 237). Seed treatment: standard processing followed by head-cutting (sirsa-kuticcaram).

medicinal_uses: Classified as Jangala-adapted (arid region) and part of the auspicious pentad (pancavrksa) of trees. In Dohada tradition, when a beautiful young woman adorned with anklet ornaments and painted with lac-dye touches the tree with her lotus-like feet, it instantaneously responds with pleasure and blooms profusely. In horticultural wonders, soil blended with fragrant asoka buds, when filled around any tree's roots, imparts a most pleasing fragrance to that tree.
dosage_preparation: Dohada: contact with ornament-adorned woman's lac-painted feet (Verse 153). Fragrance transfer: asoka bud-blended soil applied to root area of any tree (Verse 226).

medicinal_uses: Classified as Anupa-adapted (marshy/coastal region). Part of the coconut-allied palm group receiving similar nutritive treatments for fruit enhancement.
dosage_preparation: Treated alongside narikela (coconut) group with animal-flesh water irrigation and nutritive oil-cake solutions (Verses 127, 131).

medicinal_uses: Classified as Anupa-adapted (marshy/riverine region). Also recommended for directional planting in the eastern direction near residences.

Classified as Anupa-adapted (marshy region). Part of the coconut-allied palm group receiving nutritive treatments including white-mustard water, barley, paddy husk, and oil-cake water for enhanced fruit production.
dosage_preparation: Nutritive recipe: white mustard water, yava (barley), tusa (paddy husk), pinyaka (oil-cake) mixed for irrigation (Verse 131).

medicinal_uses: Classified as Anupa-adapted (marshy/coastal region). Part of the coconut-allied palm group receiving animal-flesh-and-water irrigation treatments for fruit enhancement.
dosage_preparation: Treated alongside narikela group with animal-flesh water irrigation (Verse 127).

medicinal_uses: Classified as Anupa-adapted (marshy/riverine region). For high yield, twinning grape vines should be incised and fumigated with specific fish mixed in ghee. Additionally, rooster fecal powder applied to roots and fish oil mixed with water given to roots dramatically increases fruit production. Also referenced in taste-modification recipes: grapes included among ingredients for treating bitter-tasting tree roots to produce sweet fruits.
dosage_preparation: High yield Recipe 1: incision with fumigation of saphri fish in ghee (Verse 124). Recipe 2: rooster fecal powder at roots, fish oil-water to roots (Verse 125). Taste modification: draksa with madhuka flowers, licorice, sugar, yava, and milk-kunapa irrigation for sweetening bitter fruit trees.

medicinal_uses: Classified as Sadharana-adapted (general region, suitable for common climatic conditions). Propagated by seed. Soil quality for planting is compared to the colour of campaka flowers as an ideal indicator.

Listed in general plantation principles. In Dohada tradition, Madhavi (along with karavira and kurantaka) is irrigated with sura (alcoholic liquor) in the evening in a humble manner, promoting beautiful flowering.
dosage_preparation: Dohada flowering: irrigate with sura (liquor) in evening (Verse 156).

medicinal_uses: Listed among plants in general plantation principles. Propagated by seed.

Classified as Sadharana-adapted (general region). Listed among restricted campus trees that should not be planted in residential compounds. Fruit-enhancement treatment involves irrigation with flesh mixed in water for best quality fruits.
dosage_preparation: Fruit enhancement: flesh of pherava mixed in water for irrigation (Verse 144).

medicinal_uses: Comprehensive fruit-enhancement recipe involving milk, flesh, fish, cow dung, rice, sesame-seed cake, and water applied to trees. Produces large, tasty, pulpy fruits resembling pots in size, so heavy that the tree bends under their weight.
dosage_preparation: Recipe: milk, flesh, fish, gomaya, sali rice, tila khali, and water mixed and applied (Verse 143).

medicinal_uses: Seeds are naturally curved and should be sown in their standing (curved) position. Fruit-enhancement recipe uses vidanga, masa, sesame, mustard, bilva, turmeric, and rabbit flesh mixed in water for irrigation, supplemented by pasting and fumigation treatments. Produces abundant large, juicy fruits that cause branches to bend with weight.
dosage_preparation: Seed sowing: sow in curved standing position (Section 8). Fruit enhancement: vidanga, masa, tila, sarsapa, bilva, haridra, and sataka mamsa in water for irrigation with pasting and fumigation (Verse 144).

medicinal_uses: Seeds sown for propagation; seedlings may also be planted. In horticultural wonders, stems of trapusa (cucumber) and kusmanda are pasted with honey and ghee, tied together with grass rope, covered with cow dung, and allowed to unite. When cut and grown, the resulting plant produces cucumber fruit in the size of kusmanda (ash gourd). Also used as a host for nimba seed grafting. Seedless fruit production possible by treating seeds with boar oil.
dosage_preparation: Grafting with trapusa: stems pasted with honey and ghee, tied, covered with gomaya, united, then cut and grown (Verses 265-266). Nimba grafting: nimba seed inserted in unripe kusmanda, sealed with ghee-honey, extracted after ripening (Verse 267). Seedless: seeds treated with boar oil (Verse 227).

medicinal_uses: Propagated by semi-matured stem cuttings taken from the lower portion of branches. Best propagated during the month of Kartika (post-monsoon). After planting, should be irrigated constantly for two months, keeping the propagated material nearly submerged in water. Transplanted to appropriate beds when leaves appear within two months.
dosage_preparation: Propagation: semi-matured lower stem cuttings, planted in Kartika month, kept submerged for two months, transplanted when leaves appear (Section 8, page 48).

medicinal_uses: Sown as intercropping on fertile cultivated land alongside other vegetable plants. In horticultural wonders, grafted with kusmanda by pasting stems with honey and ghee, tying together, and covering with cow dung — the united plant produces cucumber-sized fruits in ash-gourd proportions. Listed among vegetable plants receiving pig-bone fumigation during summer for enhanced production.
dosage_preparation: Grafting with kusmanda: stems pasted with honey/ghee, tied with grass rope, covered with gomaya (Verses 265-266). Vegetable fumigation: pig-bone fumigation during summer (Verse 159).

medicinal_uses: May be grown carefully on fertile and cultivated land along with maruvaka and damanaka.
Grown on fertile cultivated land. Special sowing procedure: seeds mixed with soil, irrigated with a solution of cow dung dissolved in water, then gradually watered. Best planted during Kartika month.
dosage_preparation: Sowing: seeds mixed with soil (mrda), irrigated with gomaya-water solution, watered gradually (Section 8).

medicinal_uses: Propagated by tuber. Planting haridra within the house may cause loss of progeny and wealth according to ancient authorities. Its powder (haridra curna) used as fumigation ingredient for plant treatments, including conversion of tamarind into a climber. Also used in colour-change recipes for plant fruits and flowers. Ingredient in irrigation formulations for nagarangaka fruit enhancement.
dosage_preparation: Fumigation: haridra curna for tamarind treatment (Verse 241). Colour change: part of recipes with lodhra, triphala, tila, and yastimadhu for golden-coloured fruits (Verse 236). Fruit recipes: ingredient in nagarangaka treatment (Verse 144).

medicinal_uses: Known as Krmisatru (enemy of worms), extensively used as an insecticidal agent in plant care. Seed/fruit powder applied in fumigation of seeds along with mustard. Used in seed-viability treatments: seeds are treated with honey and vidanga powder after cow-dung rubbing. Combined with ghee and curd-water for vidagdhi fumigation of plants. Ingredient in root-paste for transplanting seedlings (with honey, botanicals, and ghee). Also part of nagarangaka and kapittha fruit-enhancement recipes.
dosage_preparation: Seed fumigation: sarsapa with vidanga fumes (Verse 53). Seed viability: honey and vidanga powder treatment (Verse 54). Transplanting paste: honey, botanicals (mrsala), ghee, and vidanga powder on roots (Section 8). Vidagdhi fumigation: vidanga, ghee, curd-water, mustard, honey (Verse 122).

medicinal_uses: Best planted during the month of Caitra (spring). Listed among vegetable-fruit plants receiving pig-bone fumigation during summer season for enhanced production.
dosage_preparation: Vegetable fumigation: pig-bone fumigation during summer (Verse 159).

medicinal_uses: Part of the auspicious pentad (pancavrksa) of trees. As an indicator tree in hydrology, its presence signals suitability for cultivating mudga (green gram) crops.
Recommended for directional planting in the eastern direction near residences. Listed among trees receiving bark-paste treatment with ghee, honey, ksara, lodhra, and barley for fruit enhancement.
dosage_preparation: Bark-paste treatment: ghee, honey, ksara, lodhra, and yava applied after bark scratching (Verse 139).

medicinal_uses: Recommended for directional planting in the southern direction near residences.

One of the most frequently referenced plants in the text, used ubiquitously in preparations for plant care. Sesame is a key ingredient in seed-treatment paste (with brhati, ash, and mustard), insecticidal paste (sesame-cake with vidanga), fumigation formulations, multiple fruit-enhancement recipes (for coconut, pomegranate, nagarangaka, and others), bark-paste treatments, and colour-change preparations for fruits and flowers. Sesame oil (tila taila) and sesame-seed cake (tila khali) used extensively in plant nutrition.
dosage_preparation: Seed paste: tila with brhati, bhasma, sarsapa for seed coating (Verse 54). Insecticidal: tila khali with vidanga (Verse 101). Fumigation: tila, madhu, yava for 12-day continuous fumigation (Verse 139). Bark paste: with ghee, honey, lodhra, yava (Verse 139). Coconut recipe: saphri fish with tila powder (Verse 128). Colour change: with yava, kinkuka, manjistha for red fruits (Verse 234).
classical_citations: Vrksayurveda of Surapala, Verses 54, 101, 128, 134, 139, 234, 236; used throughout Sections 7-19

medicinal_uses: Best planted during Phalguna month (spring). Fruit yield enhanced by applying fire from dry-grass fuel during Phalguna, followed by spraying with shalika-water during Caitra. Seedless fruit production achieved by treating seeds with boar oil before sowing and irrigating with the same oil-water mixture.
dosage_preparation: Fruit yield: fire from dry grass (trnamala) in Phalguna, shalika-water spray in Caitra (Verse 160). Seedless production: seeds impregnated with boar oil, irrigated with same oil-water (Verse 227).

medicinal_uses: Best planted during Asvina month (autumn). Seedless brinjal fruits can be produced by treating seeds with boar oil (majja/vasa of krodi) before sowing, then irrigating with the same oil-water mixture.
dosage_preparation: Seedless production: seeds impregnated with boar oil, sown in land, irrigated with same oil-water mixture (Verse 227).

medicinal_uses: A key component of Triphala (with Bibhitaka and Amalaki), one of the most important formulations in the text. Triphala used extensively for fumigation and irrigation of fruit trees including panasa and dadima. Haritaki individually used in medicated irrigation mixed with honey, mustard, ghee, and nicula leaves. Part of flower colour-change recipes: mixed with kinkuka and other ingredients, irrigation with this decoction transforms white flowers to golden colour. Also used in treatment of Pitta diseases in plants as part of kvatha (decoction) with licorice.
dosage_preparation: Medicated irrigation: haritaki with honey, sarsapa, ghee, and nicula leaves (Verse 121). Triphala fumigation: haritaki, bibhitaka, amalaki powder mixed with ghee for dadima (Verse 134). Colour change: with kinkuka, karpisal, manjistha, lodhra for golden flowers (Verse 231). Pitta kvatha: with yastimadhu for plant pitta diseases.

medicinal_uses: Part of Triphala (with Haritaki and Amalaki), used extensively in plant fumigation and irrigation for fruit enhancement. Listed among vegetable-fruit plants receiving pig-bone fumigation during summer. As a hydrology indicator tree, the presence of an ant-hill adjacent to Vibhitaka signals underground water at a depth of four-and-a-half men's length.
dosage_preparation: Triphala: combined with haritaki and amalaki for fumigation and irrigation treatments. Vegetable fumigation: pig-bone fumigation during summer (Verse 159).

medicinal_uses: Licorice used in multiple plant-care formulations. In fruit enhancement, combined with sesame, honey, and kumpa for irrigating panasa (jackfruit) roots to produce bigger, ambrosia-tasting fruits. In disease treatment, part of kvatha (decoction) with haritaki for treating Pitta diseases of plants. Key ingredient in taste-modification recipes: vidanga, yastimadhu, yava, milk, and jaggery paste applied to roots of bitter-tasting trees to produce sweet fruits. Also used in fruit preservation: pills made from lotus, madhuka flowers, utpala, honey, sugar candy, and yastimadhu placed at tree roots prolong fruit-bearing duration.
dosage_preparation: Panasa root treatment: yastimadhu, tila, honey, kumpa for irrigating roots (Verse 138). Pitta treatment kvatha: with haritaki. Taste modification: vidanga, yastimadhu, yava, milk, guda paste on roots (Verse 230). Fruit preservation: pills with madhuka, utpala, honey, sugar, yastimadhu in root pit (Verse 228).

medicinal_uses: Propagated by seed and stem methods, and by branch-layering (bending down branches) with cow-dung manure and two-month irrigation until leaves appear, then incision at middle. In Dohada tradition, irrigated with sura (liquor) in the evening along with Madhavi and Kurantaka for beautiful flowering. In horticultural wonders, converted from shrub to climber by planting in a large broken pot filled with cow flesh and earth, provided with cow-dung nutrition, then transplanted into pit filled with cow bones, well-burnt ash, and water. Stems grafted with dadima for unique modifications.
dosage_preparation: Branch-layering: cow-dung manure, 2-month irrigation, incision when leaves appear. Dohada: sura irrigation in evening (Verse 156). Shrub-to-climber: planted in pot with cow flesh and earth, gomaya nutrition, transplanted to pit with bones/ash/water (Verse 240). Grafting: stems tied with dadima stems (Verse 250).

medicinal_uses: Treated with behni (paddy variety) and masa mixed in water for fruit enhancement. As a hydrology indicator tree, its presence signals suitability for growing crops maturing in 60-day periods.
dosage_preparation: Fruit treatment: behni and masa mixed in water for irrigation (Verse 142).

medicinal_uses: Used in insect-elimination paste for treating plant infestations. Combined with kustha (Saussurea lappa) and other ingredients to prepare formulations applied to affected plant parts for removing harmful insects and micro-organisms.
dosage_preparation: Insect paste: combined with kustha and other plant materials, applied to affected parts (Section 17).

medicinal_uses: Used in insect treatments for plants and in the elaborate kapittha (wood apple) seed-purification process. Roots of vaca along with dhatri, abhaya, and other plant roots are boiled in milk as part of the kapittha seed treatment that transforms the tree into a climber form.
dosage_preparation: Kapittha processing: vaca roots boiled with dhatri, abhaya, and other roots in the milk used for kapittha seed treatment (Verses 242-244). Insect treatment: combined with other plant materials (Section 17).

medicinal_uses: Used in insect-elimination paste for treating plant infestations. Combined with ativisa (Aconitum heterophyllum) and other ingredients to prepare formulations applied to affected plant parts.
dosage_preparation: Insect paste: combined with ativisa and other materials, applied to affected plant parts (Section 17).

medicinal_uses: Used in colour-change recipes for plant flowers and fruits. When combined with darada (hingula), milk, and other minerals, and pasted over tree roots, transforms white flowers to golden colour. Also part of recipes for producing red-coloured fruits when combined with yava, kinkuka, nisa, and tila.
dosage_preparation: Golden flowers: manjistha, darada, milk, pidavana pasted on roots (Verse 232). Red fruits: with yava, kinkuka, nisa, tila ground in water for paste and irrigation (Verse 234). White-to-golden: with haritaki, kinkuka, karpisal, lodhra (Verse 231).

medicinal_uses: Powder used in bark-paste treatment for fruit trees along with yava (barley), applied after bark scratching for fruit enhancement. Also part of colour-change recipes: combined with triphala, tila, yastimadhu, haridra, and nilika for producing golden-coloured fruits. Used with haritaki and other ingredients for transforming white flowers to golden colour.
dosage_preparation: Bark paste: lodhra and yava powder with ghee, honey, ksara applied to scratched bark (Verse 139). Golden fruit colour: with triphala, tila, yastimadhu, haridra, nilika as powder/spray on roots (Verse 236). Golden flowers: with haritaki, kinkuka, karpisal, manjistha for decoction irrigation (Verse 231).

medicinal_uses: Listed among restricted campus trees not suitable for planting in residential compounds. Bark used in wound-healing paste for damaged plants. As a hydrology indicator tree, its presence signals rainfall (vrsti). Also referenced in fumigation formulations where its flower (Partha) is used as an ingredient.
dosage_preparation: Wound paste: arjuna bark combined with other barks, applied to tree wounds (Section 17). Fumigation ingredient: arjuna flower (Partha) in plant fumigation powder (Verse 119).

medicinal_uses: Barley is one of the most frequently used ingredients in plant-care formulations. Barley flour (yavacurna) used in coconut fruit-enhancement recipes with alkaline water and paddy husk. Part of bark-paste treatment (with lodhra) for fruit trees. Ingredient in plant disease treatments including anaemia (paleness) cures and sterility remedies for non-fruiting trees. Used in kapittha seed-processing irrigation (with masa, tila, honey, and fish-water). Key ingredient in 12-day continuous fumigation with sesame and honey for fruit trees. Part of colour-change recipes for producing red-coloured fruits.
dosage_preparation: Coconut recipe: yavacurna with alkaline water, paddy husk, pulse soup (Verse 130). Bark paste: with lodhra, ghee, honey, ksara (Verse 139). Fumigation: yava, tila, madhu for 12 days (Verse 139). Kapittha irrigation: with masa, tila, honey, fish-water (Verses 242-244). Red fruits: with kinkuka, manjistha, nisa, tila (Verse 234).

medicinal_uses: Used in treatment of trees damaged by thunderbolts (lightning strikes). Part of restorative formulations applied to severely damaged trees to aid recovery.
dosage_preparation: Thunderbolt-damage treatment: combined with other plant materials in restorative formulations (Section 17).

medicinal_uses: Listed among restricted campus trees not suitable for planting in residential compounds. In horticultural wonders, castor seeds treated with pig fat (kokara vasa) and sown in suitable land reportedly produce fruits resembling karkati (a type of melon/cucumber) rather than typical castor fruits. Seeds also given special wrapping treatment with jaggery solution and heating for three days to enhance viability.
dosage_preparation: Habit modification: seeds treated with pig fat, sown in land (Verse 239). Seed viability: seeds wrapped with plaster and jaggery solution, heated for 3 days (Section 7).

medicinal_uses: In horticultural wonders, the tamarind tree can be converted from its normal tree form into a climber by treating it with a recipe of Triphala powder, sesame, barley, and masa mixed in water, followed by fumigation with turmeric powder. The transformed plant continues flowering regularly.
dosage_preparation: Tree-to-climber conversion: irrigate with triphala powder, tila, yava, masa in water; fumigate with haridra curna (Verse 241).

medicinal_uses: Listed among restricted campus trees not suitable for planting in residential compounds. As a hydrology indicator, Kancana (Kovidara) growing near specific landmarks indicates the presence of underground water sources.

Oil extracted from ankola seeds extensively used for seed-impregnation treatments. Seeds soaked in ankola oil (combined with fish, sisumara, and crocodile oils) and dried produce plants that flower and fruit simultaneously. Ankola fruit juice also used in mango fruit-enhancement recipe along with ghee, honey, and animal fat. Seeds soaked in ankola oil seven times and dried produce rapid germination when irrigated with coconut water.
dosage_preparation: Seed impregnation: seeds soaked in ankola oil, dried, sown, irrigated with coconut water for rapid germination (Verses 259-263). Mango enhancement: ankola fruit juice with ghee, honey, animal fat in water for root treatment (Verse 126).

medicinal_uses: Propagated by seed and stem methods. In Dohada tradition, mallika responds to gentle warming with fire produced from burning grasses, which promotes profuse flowering that attracts honey-bees constantly.
dosage_preparation: Dohada flowering: light warming with fire from grass fuel (Verse 157).

medicinal_uses: Listed among restricted campus trees that should not be planted in residential compounds. Recommended for directional planting in the southern direction. Propagated by seed. Listed among trees receiving bark-paste treatment with ghee, honey, ksara, lodhra, and barley for fruit enhancement. As a hydrology indicator, the presence of an ant-hill adjacent to Badara signals ground water in the western direction at three hands' distance and three men's depth.
dosage_preparation: Bark-paste treatment: ghee, honey, ksara, lodhra, yava applied after bark scratching (Verse 139).

medicinal_uses: Classified among Anupa-adapted (marshy region) plants. Flowering enhanced by irrigating with aromatic decoction prepared from eladi gandha (aromatic herbal oils) mixed with flesh-water. Alternatively, irrigation with honey-water and other waste-liquid mixtures promotes high flower yield.
dosage_preparation: Flowering Recipe 1: decoction of eladi gandha aromatics in flesh-water for irrigation (Verse 150). Recipe 2: honey-water (maksha jala) irrigation.

medicinal_uses: Propagated by seed-tuber method. Root rhizomes of different lotus varieties extracted, tied together with thread, pasted with ghee and honey, then planted — resulting in blooming of multiple flower colours on a single plant. Seeds used in fruit-preservation pills combined with madhuka flowers, utpala, honey, sugar candy, and yastimadhu. Featured prominently in garden pond design alongside nilotpala (blue lotus). Lotus seed treatment with ajagara skin and mud produces lotus flower gardens.
dosage_preparation: Multi-colour experiment: rhizomes of different varieties tied, pasted with ghee-honey, planted (Verse 249). Preservation pills: kamala, madhuka, utpala, honey, sugar, yastimadhu in root pit (Verse 228). Garden production: seeds in ajagara skin, sown in mud, irrigated with flesh-water (Verse 269).

medicinal_uses: Used as an ingredient in decoction (along with jambu leaves and sura liquor) for irrigating all flowering plants to promote maximum bloom and flower yield.
dosage_preparation: Flowering decoction: mustaka with kosira, jambu leaves in sura (liquor) for irrigation of flowering plants (Verse 149).

medicinal_uses: In Dohada tradition, Patala tree should be irrigated with milk mixed with water to promote profuse flowering that attracts honey-bees. As a hydrology indicator tree, its presence signals availability of cows in the area.
dosage_preparation: Dohada flowering: irrigate with milk-water mixture (Verse 157).

medicinal_uses: Listed among tuber-propagated plants in the plant classification system.
Listed among seed-tuber propagated plants in the plant classification system.

Referenced in the spiritual merit section: planting along with dadima, rambha, priyala, and panasa prevents sorrow for seven births. Seeds treated under standard processing and dried with head-cutting for improved viability. Recommended for planting during Sravana month.
dosage_preparation: Seed treatment: standard processing followed by head-cutting (sirsa-kuticcaram) for viability (Section 7).

medicinal_uses: Planting Nila within the house may cause loss of progeny and wealth. If such plants grow naturally on their own within the house, they are considered harmful. This is the view of well-versed authorities.
Sacred grass referenced in hydrology section: land having growth of munja, kusa (darbha), and other grasses with blue soil keeps underground water sources that provide tasty water in abundance.
Referenced in hydrology section: land with growth of munja reed, kusa grass, and blue soil indicates underground water sources providing tasty water in abundance.
Listed among vegetable-fruit plants receiving pig-bone fumigation during summer for enhanced production.
dosage_preparation: Vegetable fumigation: pig-bone fumigation during summer (Verse 159).

medicinal_uses: Leaves and roots used for providing manure (nutrition) to Madhuka trees, contributing to the production of camphor-fragrant flowers.
dosage_preparation: Manure: leaves and roots of Jaalini given as samskrta (nutrition) to madhuka tree (Verse 146).

medicinal_uses: Paddy referenced extensively throughout the text. Paddy husk (tusa) used in multiple formulations: coconut fruit enhancement, fumigation recipes, and soil-covering treatments. Rice-boiled watery part (manda) used for irrigating certain plants for high fruit yield. Paddy-husk ash (tusa bhasma) combined with cow dung as banana manure. Rope made from rice straw used in stem-cutting propagation.
dosage_preparation: Coconut recipe: paddy husk with alkaline water, barley flour, pulse soup (Verse 130). Banana manure: tusa bhasma with gomaya (Verse 141). Rice-water irrigation: tandula manda for high fruit yield (Verse 159).
classical_citations: Vrksayurveda of Surapala, Verses 130, 141, 159; used throughout

medicinal_uses: Valued in Northeast Indian tribal medicine for its sedative, analgesic, and antispasmodic properties. Employed to treat digestive disorders, fever, and respiratory conditions.
dosage_preparation: Preparations typically use the rhizome; specific dosage not detailed in the paper.

medicinal_uses: 1. Bark decoction (1/4 to 3/4 tola) taken three times a day mixed in half a cup of warm water reduces cough and phlegm. 2. Bark powder is applied on wounds after grinding with turmeric to promote healing. 3. Agase leaf stalk extract is used as nasal drops to relieve headache; similarly the extract applied to the eyes helps with eye irritation issues. 4. Consuming agase leaf juice mixed with a little pepper helps relieve body aches. 5. Inhaling agase leaf juice through the nose (4-5 drops per nostril) helps cure various types of fever. 6. Agase leaf juice (4-6 spoons) taken with a little honey, twice daily for one week, helps relieve urinary problems. 7. Decoction of the leaves with ginger and dry ginger, consumed warm, provides relief from stomach pain. 8. Sniffing flower juice excessively through the nose helps reduce nasal congestion. 9. Flower juice combined with raw turmeric decoction used as a vaginal wash helps prevent vaginal infections. 10. A mixture of agase seeds (1 tola), gasagase (1 tola), and sugar (1 tola) ground together, made into pills and applied as a poultice, treats groin boils (Groin bubo).
dosage_preparation: Bark decoction: 1/4 to 3/4 tola in half cup warm water, three times daily. Leaf juice: 4-6 spoons with honey, twice daily for one week. In veterinary use, agase leaves are fed to cattle twice daily for 40 days to address pregnancy-related complications.

medicinal_uses: 1. Root and leaf decoction is consumed to treat edema and swelling-related conditions. 2. The plant is used in managing abdominal disorders including gastric problems, enlarged spleen, and liver conditions. 3. The root decoction is taken during menstrual irregularities, specifically for treating painful periods (Dysmenorrhoea). Dosage: 14-28 mL decoction. 4. It is beneficial in treating urinary problems and kidney-related ailments. 5. The fresh plant juice or decoction helps manage jaundice and other liver disorders. 6. External application of leaf paste helps treat skin diseases and boils. 7. Root preparation is used as an antidote in some toxicity conditions. 8. The plant is considered useful in managing heart conditions and as a general tonic.
dosage_preparation: Root decoction: 14-28 mL taken with appropriate adjuvants. The plant also has a variety (B. chinensis) that is mentioned alongside the main species.

medicinal_uses: 1. Drinking the decoction of Annesoppu root helps treat cannabis-induced intoxication and related nervous disorders. 2. Consuming leaf juice or decoction with bitter gourd helps treat liver disorders (Liver) and urinary tract diseases. 3. Drinking water in which the seeds have been soaked helps stop diarrhea. 4. Consuming the outer shell or seeds of the plant after grinding helps improve virility and reproductive health. 5. Grinding the seeds into a paste and applying it to the eyes helps treat eye diseases and improves vision.
dosage_preparation: Root decoction taken orally for intoxication. Seeds soaked in water for diarrhea. Seed paste applied externally for eye conditions.

medicinal_uses: 1. Condensed decoction or kashaya of Amruta Balli treats diabetes effectively. 2. Amruta Balli churna, kashaya, and satva are important Ayurvedic preparations; it is recognized in Ayurvedic texts as an excellent Rejuvenator. 3. Amruta Balli juice (1.5-2 spoons) taken with a little honey on an empty stomach daily for one week cures Jaundice. 4. Amruta Balli juice (approximately 1/2 tola) with a little honey, taken 2-3 times daily, cures chronic fever. 5. Amruta Balli or leaf juice (1.25 tola) with a little sugar, taken twice daily for 3-5 days, cures bleeding disorders. 6. The vine stem powder mixed with milk and consumed helps enhance virility. 7. Root ground into paste and applied to joints helps with joint swelling. 8. Vine stem extract (7-14 mL) with a little honey, taken twice daily, cures leprosy and skin diseases. 9. Vine stem decoction (14-28 mL) with Guggul (2 grams), taken twice daily, cures Arthritis. 10. A combination of Amruta Balli, dry ginger, and neem leaves in equal quantities, ground into churna (2 spoons per liter of water), boiled and consumed overnight for 5-6 days, cures flu and influenza. 11. Amruta Balli juice (1 tola with 1 tola honey) taken every night helps relieve phlegm/mucus conditions. 12. Amruta Balli and ginger in equal quantities, dried and ground into churna (2 tola), made into decoction and consumed twice daily for 20-40 days, cures Hysteria. 13. A combined decoction of Amruta Balli, Adusoge, and honey (each 1/4 tola) taken four times daily for 2-3 months cures tuberculosis (Kshaya). 14. Amruta Balli leaf or fruit juice applied on the face nightly helps improve complexion and prevent wrinkles. 15. Amruta Balli juice (2-3 mL) with honey, consumed during food poisoning from contaminated food, helps relieve food poisoning symptoms; treatment should continue for several months.
dosage_preparation: Juice: 1.5-2 spoons with honey on empty stomach for jaundice (1 week). Half tola with honey 2-3 times daily for chronic fever. Stem decoction: 14-28 mL with 2g Guggul for arthritis. Churna: 2 tola in decoction for hysteria (20-40 days). In veterinary use, Amruta Balli root is ground, mixed with 1 liter of water, and administered to cattle orally and applied on wounds.

medicinal_uses: 1. Bark dried, powdered and made into decoction; consuming it with jaggery helps treat snakebite, scorpion sting, and other venomous bites. The treatment involves taking the bark decoction with jaggery, sugar, or honey at specific intervals (4 times in 16 hours, then 2 times in 3 hours, then 2 hours, then 3 hours each). 2. Leaf juice applied on wounds helps heal them quickly. 3. Bark ash mixed with water is used for cleaning teeth and treating dental problems. 4. Bark decoction with ghee, jaggery, curd, milk, and honey is consumed as a health tonic. 5. Aerial root decoction helps treat excessive menstrual bleeding (Leucorrhoea) and urinary disorders. 6. Bark is used medicinally to treat disorders related to vata imbalance. 7. Bark preparations are effective in managing various conditions. 8. Bark decoction taken 2-3 times a day helps control diarrhea. 9. Combination of bark, turmeric, ginger, and other ingredients in specified proportions helps manage piles; fresh bark juice (1/2 tola) with sugar (1/4 tola) taken for 14-21 days provides relief from Piles. 10. Bark, leaves, aerial roots, and fruits combined are used as a general health tonic. 11. Dried Arali leaves consumed with appropriate adjuvants help manage various skin conditions, ringworm, and eczema (Eczema). 12. Bark decoction combined with other herbs treats respiratory diseases. 13. Bark preparations combined with Arali leaves help treat scorpion stings and other venomous bites.
dosage_preparation: Bark decoction: various dosages specified for different conditions. For piles: fresh bark juice 1/2 tola with sugar 1/4 tola for 14-21 days. Bark powder: 12 tola boiled with water, taken 3-4 times daily. Various combinations with jaggery, sugar, honey, ghee, and other herbs for different ailments.

medicinal_uses: 1. Leaf juice applied on the scalp helps treat dandruff and headache. 2. Gum (Bondi) dissolved in buttermilk and consumed helps treat diarrhea and blood-related disorders. 3. Gum (Bondi) dissolved in water, mixed with alum, and used as a gargle helps relieve throat and gum pain. 4. Leaf and flower juice preparations act as Stimulants (stimulating agents) for the body. 5. Flowers are used to prepare a dye of yellow and reddish hue for coloring purposes.
dosage_preparation: Leaf juice applied topically on the scalp. Gum dissolved in buttermilk for diarrhea. Gum dissolved in water with alum as a gargle solution.

latin_name: Saraca asoca (Roxb.) de Wilde = S. indica auct non L.
medicinal_uses: 1. Bark and flower preparations help manage menstrual disorders and excessive uterine bleeding. The bark is described as sacred in Hindu tradition, associated with Sita in Ramayana and with Buddha's birthplace. 2. Bark decoction combined with other ingredients helps treat uterine disorders. 3. Bark and flower medicinal preparations help in managing gynecological conditions. 4. The bark helps manage skin diseases and complexion-related issues. 5. Bark preparations help treat bleeding disorders. 6. The bark is also useful in treating internal inflammation. 7. Bark preparations are given for various women's health conditions including menorrhagia, leucorrhoea, and other gynecological problems.
dosage_preparation: Bark decoction prepared with water. Various bark and flower preparations used for gynecological conditions. The tree grows 20-30 feet tall and is commonly used as an ornamental tree in gardens.

latin_name: Tylophora indica (N. Burman) Merrill = T. asthmatica (L.f.) Wight & Arn.
medicinal_uses: 1. Consuming leaves and root helps treat wheezing, asthma, and blood-related disorders. (Note: This plant should not be used for inducing vomiting; it is suitable only for respiratory diseases.) 2. Adumuttada Balli root (1/4 liter), turmeric (20 grams), and coconut oil (1/4 liter) boiled together, stored in a bottle, and the oil applied to the nose daily helps cure sinusitis and related conditions; continued treatment over several days shows improvement. 3. Adumuttada Balli root juice (15-20 drops) put in warm milk and consumed helps treat pertussis (whooping cough). 4. Adumuttada Balli root (1 tola) and leaf (1 tola) boiled, filtered, and given to patients in moderate doses (during the 6th to 9th month of pregnancy) induces vomiting and helps with various health conditions. Caution: Food should be plain rice.
dosage_preparation: Root oil preparation: 1/4 liter root juice with 20g turmeric and 1/4 liter coconut oil, boiled and used as nasal drops. Root juice: 15-20 drops in warm milk for whooping cough. In veterinary use: Adumuttada Balli leaf powder (2 tola), Lakshmi Soppu (1 tola), Tumbe Soppu (2 tola), Palavaanada Soppu (2 tola), Karimenu (1/2 tola), Belluli (1 tola) ground together in buttermilk and given twice daily for 3 days to treat cattle head-related diseases.

latin_name: Adhatoda zeylanica Medic. = A. vasica Nees
medicinal_uses: 1. Root decoction taken by grinding helps children expel phlegm and relieves cough. 2. Leaf juice (14-28 mL) taken with a little honey, three times daily, treats cough, fever, and respiratory tract diseases. 3. Leaf juice (14-28 mL) with a little honey, taken once daily, cures leucorrhoea (white vaginal discharge). 4. Leaf juice (7-14 mL) with honey, taken twice daily, stops bleeding and helps with blood-related disorders. 5. Leaves ground with turmeric and gomutra (cow urine), applied on the face, helps treat pimples and other skin conditions. 6. Adusoge leaf juice (2 tola) with 2 spoons honey, taken nightly on an empty stomach, helps reduce phlegm-related diseases. 7. Leaves ground and applied as a poultice on burning areas or inflamed joints provides relief from pain and swelling. 8. Leaf juice or decoction helps treat bleeding piles (hemorrhoids); fresh leaves applied as a poultice on the affected area of piles helps reduce pain and swelling. 9. Flower powder (1/2-1 gram) taken with suitable adjuvant, twice daily, stops blood-related bleeding. 10. Flower juice or decoction helps treat strangury (Strangury) and oliguria (Oliguria).
dosage_preparation: Leaf juice: 14-28 mL with honey, 3 times daily for cough. 7-14 mL with honey twice daily for bleeding. Flower powder: 1/2-1 gram with adjuvant twice daily. Note: Leaf juice when heated in a pan produces green leaves that can be stored and reconstituted with water.

medicinal_uses: 1. Root used for dental care; chewing root serves as a toothbrush and treats various dental problems. 2. Plant has properties useful for treating skin diseases, hemorrhoids, and abdominal ailments. 3. Root preparations used for treating urinary disorders and kidney-related conditions. 4. Root used for treating snake bite by grinding and applying externally. 5. The plant helps in treating dropsy and other swelling conditions. 6. Root and leaf preparations (8 tola root, 12 ratti leaves, and other ingredients) in specified proportions, used 3-4 times daily, help with various conditions. 7. Root is administered with milk (1-2 tola) for treating anemia (Anaemia); treatment continued for 5-7 days. 8. Plant preparations help manage respiratory conditions including asthma and bronchitis. Leaves smoked help with respiratory relief; dosage is 2 times initially then 2-3 times later. 9. Root preparations combined with ginger and other herbs treat joint pain and rheumatic conditions. 10. Root and plant ash is used in various external applications for skin conditions (3-4 inch length pieces used in preparations). 11. A combination of the root with turmeric, cumin, black pepper (4 each), camphor (1 ratti), honey (1 tola) helps treat skin diseases and eczema (Eczema). 12. Root combined with salt, pepper, and other spices used in preparations, applied or taken internally for 10 days. 13. Specific preparations taken (2 tola with 1/4 tola other ingredients) for digestive conditions. 14. Plant preparations are used in treating Gonorrhoea. 15. Root powder (3 tola) taken with milk (1/2 tola) mixed with sugar, and consuming the decoction helps treat headaches and migraines. 16. Root powder (3 tola) taken with palm jaggery (1/2 tola) helps treat irregular menstruation. 17. The plant is used in treating piles by preparing a suppository-like application for external use. 18. Flower preparations mixed with milk help stop premature termination of pregnancy. 19. Single seed taken with salt water daily for 7 days helps with blood-related conditions and bleeding piles.
dosage_preparation: Root: 1-2 tola with milk for anemia (5-7 days). Root powder: 3 tola with 1/2 tola palm jaggery for menstrual irregularity. Single seed with salt water for 7 days for piles. In veterinary use, Uttarane Soppu and Sondala root preparations administered to cattle for various conditions.

medicinal_uses: 1. Root juice or decoction mixed with honey is given to those suffering from stomach ache and intestinal colic. 2. Root ground and mixed with butter and salt water is consumed for treating excess bile and reducing excessive salivation. 3. If poison has been consumed and vomiting is needed, root is ground and consumed with buttermilk. 4. Root or stem bark of Uppimullu is ground and applied as a poultice on boils and swollen areas. 5. Root, leaf, and fruit bark preparations taken with churna help treat various conditions including skin and joint diseases. 6. Leaf decoction or churna consumed helps reduce cough and phlegm-related diseases. 7. Leaf kashaya or decoction helps treat skin conditions, rashes, and itching. 8. Consuming the fresh juice of the plant helps treat stomach and digestive disorders and provides relief from constipation. 9. Preparing Uppimullu leaf decoction and consuming it regularly over time helps treat bone and joint-related diseases. 10. To repel bad odors and insects, the plant leaves are eaten at night before going to bed.
dosage_preparation: Root juice with honey for stomach ache. Root with buttermilk for inducing vomiting in poisoning cases. Leaf decoction consumed regularly for joint diseases. In veterinary use: Uppimullu leaf powder (1 tola), Bagamara bark (chakke), Kamanasina (soppu mukkyalu tola), Palavaanada Soppu (2 tola), Karimenu (2 tola), Belluli (1 tola) ground in buttermilk, taken twice daily for narada (gullaroga) treatment.

latin_name: Carmona retusa (Vahl) Masamune = Ehretia microphylla Lamk.
medicinal_uses: 1. Consuming the root helps improve body functions and is used to treat conditions where the body's normal processes are disrupted; also helps manage post-poisoning effects. 2. Leaf juice is used for treating red spots on the skin; applied along with lemon juice from the outside (for conditions caused by skin problems), it helps reduce redness and pain.
dosage_preparation: Root consumed for systemic conditions. Leaf juice applied with lemon juice externally for skin redness.

latin_name: Alangium salvifolium (L.f.) Wang. = A. lamarckii Thw.
medicinal_uses: 1. Root mixed with sesame oil or hemp oil and applied to the gums with honey helps stop diarrhea. 2. Root decoction or paste applied on a dog bite helps neutralize the venom/toxin. 3. An oil preparation from the bark is used for treating various skin conditions and external infections. 4. Root bark churna (4 tola) made into decoction, consumed for 7 days, is said to act as an artificial poison antidote (the text notes multiple types of toxic conditions and poisons it treats, including dental, hair, and skin conditions, but cautions that this is a strong medicine). 5. Root bark decoction (5-10 gunje) consumed helps reduce fever. 6. Ankole seed churna consumed for several days helps build physical strength and vitality. 7. Ankole seed churna mixed with honey, consumed alongside antidotes, helps treat snake poison.
dosage_preparation: Root bark churna: 4 tola in decoction for 7 days. Root bark decoction: 5-10 gunje for fever. Seed churna with honey for snake poison. In veterinary use: Ankole root bark boiled, used as poultice, and decoction given for 2-3 days to treat digestive and skin diseases in cattle.

latin_name: Sapindus laurifolia Vahl. = S. trifoliatus Hiern
medicinal_uses: 1. A combination of Antuvala seeds and shell powder mixed with a little salt, made into pills, taken once or twice daily for 1-2 weeks, treats cough and cold. 2. Antuvala fruit ground and applied on Scrofula (glandular swelling), helps reduce swelling over time. 3. Antuvala fruit ground and applied along with turmeric and other herbs on infected skin areas helps treat skin conditions and neutralize poison effects. 4. Antuvala fruit prepared as churna and consumed helps treat urinary tract diseases and related conditions. 5. Applying the fruit on skin lesions helps reduce the symptoms of skin diseases that appear on the surface. 6. Antuvala fruit churna or Akshemu (a preparation) consumed helps reduce digestive problems and appetite-related issues. 7. Rubbing the fruit on the body and adding vinegar (Vinegar) to the head as a wash helps eliminate head lice. 8. Antuvala fruit soaked in water and applied on the nose helps soften hardened phlegm, aiding in expulsion of cough and reducing throat congestion.
dosage_preparation: Seed and shell pills with salt: once or twice daily for 1-2 weeks for cough. Fruit paste applied externally for skin conditions. Fruit churna consumed for urinary tract conditions. Fruit soaked in water applied nasally for phlegm.

medicinal_uses: 1. Andarugida leaves, Mangaravalli balli, and Komottaiya (type of plant) leaves combined, ground, and applied as a paste on the forehead and behind the ears on the face helps cure headaches within 8-10 days. 2. Leaf churna applied on wounds helps accelerate wound healing; the wound will not turn septic and the wound will heal without scarring. 3. Leaves ground and applied on a snake bite area along with consuming the preparation internally helps neutralize snake venom. 4. Leaf decoction or kashaya taken with milk and consumed helps cure fever and general body conditions. 5. Leaf decoction or kashaya consumed orally helps reduce fever.
dosage_preparation: Leaf paste applied on forehead for headache. Leaf churna applied on wounds. Leaf decoction with milk for fever. Some cultivate the plant as a hedge. Dried branches are used to make brooms for sweeping and as fuel.

medicinal_uses: 1. Root paste mixed with a small amount of turmeric and sugar, taken orally for several days, helps treat various urinary disorders and Polyuria. 2. Bark decoction with a little honey taken for several days helps treat Psoriasis and other skin diseases (Charma roga). 3. Raw fruit of Kakke mara made into a paste with oil, applied on skin eruptions, helps treat various skin diseases. 4. Ripe fruit of Kakke mara ground to 1 tola of powder, taken with warm milk for 3 days, helps heal wounds and promote faster recovery. 5. Kakke mara pulp ground with turmeric, mixed with honey, and applied on the head helps treat syphilis (Syphilis) and other venereal diseases. 6. Root or leaf extract applied on swollen glands helps reduce swelling; when applied as a poultice on joints, it helps reduce Rheumatic and arthritic pain. 7. Fresh bark juice mixed with salt water or dissolved in buttermilk, applied externally 2-3 times, helps treat ringworm, skin rashes, and eczema-like conditions. 8. About 12-24 grams of Kakke leaf paste mixed with ghee, taken daily 4 times, helps treat rheumatic joint disease (Sandhivaata roga). 9. Kakke bark ground and placed on the front teeth (as a chewing preparation), applied twice daily with 1/2 spoon sugar and 1/2 spoon honey, consumed for 1-2 weeks, helps reduce blood impurities and related ailments. 10. Using the fruit to prepare a chemical called Rhein (a natural product), which is useful in treating skin diseases, dermatitis, piles, and urinary tract diseases. 11. Kakke fruit peel ground and prepared, mixed with a little ghee, helps cure cough. 12. Kakke bark powder (approximately 5 grams) with suitable sugar and water, taken daily, helps reduce constipation. 13. Kakke churna (1/4 tola) taken with milk, consumed daily (once in the morning and once at night), helps reduce stomach discomfort and serves as a poison antidote; treatment duration is several days.
dosage_preparation: Root paste with turmeric and sugar for urinary disorders. Bark decoction with honey for psoriasis. Fruit powder: 1 tola with warm milk for 3 days. Leaf paste: 12-24 grams with ghee, 4 times daily for rheumatic conditions. Bark powder: 5 grams with sugar and water for constipation. Churna: 1/4 tola with milk daily. In veterinary use: Kakke fruit is cut, ground, and made into a watery decoction (1 tola in water) given to cattle for 3-4 days to treat digestive and diarrheal conditions.

latin_name: Anthocephalus indicus A. Rich. = A. cadamba Miq.
medicinal_uses: 1. Bark decoction consumed helps reduce fever due to its antipyretic properties. 2. Bark juice and extract in equal quantities consumed together with relevant adjuvants helps treat eye conditions; consuming in appropriate quantities with salt helps with certain conditions. 3. Eating the fruits produced by the tree helps treat fever associated with wasting. 4. Kadavala bark juice mixed with salt and sugar consumed helps cure stomach and digestive ailments. 5. Kadavala fruit mixed with churna and taken with honey helps with various conditions and benefits reproductive health (Kushtha). 6. Kadavala bark churna consumed with godhuma (wheat) as adjuvant helps treat vata-related diseases. 7. Kadavala bark ground to powder, made into churna with appropriate additives, and consumed for about 6 months helps manage various chronic conditions.
dosage_preparation: Bark decoction for fever. Bark juice with extract for eye conditions. Fruit with churna and honey. Bark churna with wheat for vata diseases. Long-term use: bark churna consumed for about 6 months for chronic conditions.

latin_name: Nerium indicum Miller = N. odorum Soland.
medicinal_uses: WARNING: This plant is highly poisonous. It should never be consumed carelessly or in excessive quantities as it may cause death. All traditional medical practices should be carried out under supervision of a qualified practitioner. 1. Root ground and applied on hemorrhoids (piles) and boils on the skin helps reduce swelling. 2. Root ground and applied in the vaginal area or applied as a suppository to the cervix helps prevent unwanted pregnancies (acts as an abortifacient). 3. Bark decoction taken in appropriate quantities mixed with mustard oil, applied as a massage, helps treat leprosy (Kushtha). 4. Leaf juice combined with mustard oil and coconut oil, prepared as a medicated oil preparation, helps treat itching and skin conditions. 5. Kanagile leaves placed in a pot, heated, and the concentrated juice applied on hair roots as a drip treatment helps treat warts (Wart). 6. Kanagile leaf extract combined with honey and black pepper applied as a paste and wrapped tightly helps reduce the speed of boil growth. 7. Leaves ground and applied on ringworm (Ring Worm) patches using a coconut shell as applicator helps treat ringworm.
dosage_preparation: Root paste applied externally for piles. Bark decoction with mustard oil for leprosy. Leaf juice with oils for skin conditions. All preparations should be used with extreme caution due to the poisonous nature of the plant. In veterinary use: A single leaf is cut, roasted on hot coals, and the juice given to cattle for various conditions.

medicinal_uses: 1. Consuming ripe fruit helps cure bleeding disorders (Raktamoolavyadhi). 2. Fruit sherbet prepared by crushing the fruit and drinking it helps reduce fever-related excessive thirst. 3. Consuming the fruit helps stop diarrhea and vomiting. 4. Fruit squeezed and the resulting churna (juice/powder preparation) consumed helps reduce fever.
dosage_preparation: Ripe fruit consumed directly for bleeding disorders. Fruit sherbet for fever-related thirst. Fruit consumed for diarrhea and vomiting. Fruit juice preparation for fever reduction.

medicinal_uses: 1. Tuber (gadde) paste applied externally and taken internally in controlled doses helps treat snake bites and venomous stings. 2. Preparations from the plant help in treating skin conditions and various inflammatory diseases. 3. The tuber is used in specific quantities to induce labor and address gynecological conditions (it has abortifacient properties). 4. Tuber preparations applied on the body help manage various conditions including parasitic infections. 5. A preparation using the tuber in specified quantities (like 250 mg or 500 mg) combined with appropriate carriers helps treat gout-related and arthritic conditions. 6. The plant has applications in traditional medicine but must be used cautiously due to its toxic properties (Colchicine content).
dosage_preparation: Tuber paste for snake bites (external and internal). Small controlled doses of tuber preparations (250-500 mg) with suitable adjuvants for gout and arthritis. WARNING: This plant contains colchicine and is highly toxic. Dosage of 4-5 leaves can be lethal. Must be used only under expert supervision. The tuber size is typically 3 cm, and the plant grows as a tendril climber in evergreen and semi-deciduous forests.

latin_name: Datura metel L. (= D. fastuosa L.)
medicinal_uses: 1. Boiling the root of the datura plant in water and drinking the decoction helps treat poisonous bites. 2. Warming datura leaves in milk or oil and applying on swollen areas or painful joints provides relief; heating leaves with castor oil and applying at night on swollen joints reduces inflammation; consuming a decoction of leaves with cumin helps induce sleep and reduce insomnia. 3. Using purified datura root and bark with ingredients like camphor and painting-related substances is described in traditional texts. 4. A mixture of leaf juice (4 parts) and sesame oil (1 part) heated together and filtered can be used as nasal drops. 5. Mixing 350 mL leaf juice with 140 mL sesame oil, boiling with turmeric (2 grams) and wax, then filtering and storing in a bottle can be applied to treat burns, wounds, and various skin diseases; this oil also stops itching. 6. Grinding the leaves, roasting them with sesame oil and applying as a poultice relieves asthma and breathing difficulties. 7. Fruits of datura are dried, cut open, and the seeds removed; the pulp is made into a paste and applied to swollen areas (including mumps) to reduce swelling within 3-4 days. 8. Seeds and dried fruit powder mixed in equal proportions with turmeric and sesame oil can be applied to treat liver spots (chloasma). 9. Leaves can be ground and applied as a poultice on mumps (parotid swelling) to reduce inflammation within 5-6 days.
dosage_preparation: Leaf juice: 350 mL mixed with 140 mL sesame oil and 2g turmeric for oil preparation. For veterinary use: seeds 5g, bark of Terminalia arjuna 10g, neem bark 10g, ginger powder 10g mixed and given for 4-5 days to treat animal diseases.

medicinal_uses: 1. Chewing the bark of the plant like sugarcane and consuming the juice helps manage bleeding disorders and blood-related symptoms. 2. Grinding the bark and making it into a paste with gandha (fragrance-producing substance) applied as body paste helps treat skin diseases. 3. Bathing with a decoction of the bark helps manage skin diseases and various parasitic skin infections. 4. Consuming a decoction of bark or its powder helps treat diarrhea, bleeding, and stops excessive bleeding; it also purifies blood. 5. The bark powder consumed with appropriate vehicle helps treat respiratory ailments and asthma. 6. A decoction of bark and fruit helps prevent uterine disorders. 7. Consuming bark powder helps treat urinary tract infections and provides relief from urethral inflammation. 8. Grinding the thorns and collecting the gum from the tree to consume with appropriate vehicle provides various health benefits.
dosage_preparation: Bark decoction and powder are the main preparations; applied externally as paste or taken internally as decoction.

latin_name: Centratherum anthelminticum (Willd.) O.Kuntze (= Vernonia anthelminticum Willd.)
medicinal_uses: 1. Grinding the leaves and roots and applying the paste on joints and wounds acts as an anti-inflammatory and wound healer. 2. Applying a paste of leaves and roots on the skin helps treat poisonous conditions and bites. 3. Applying the leaf juice on the scalp helps relieve headaches and body heat. 4. Soaking seeds in water and consuming helps treat heat-related conditions, body pain, and also addresses various skin diseases. 5. Mixing seeds (4 parts) with yellow orpiment/haritala (1 part) and grinding them together in cow's urine, then applying, helps treat skin conditions. 6. Taking 8 grams each of seeds and pepper, soaking in water, and drinking helps cure fever, and treats all types of skin diseases. 7. Consuming the plant helps cleanse the uterus and also promotes lactation. 8. Soaking seeds in water overnight, straining, and applying to the face in the morning gives a glowing complexion.
dosage_preparation: Seeds: 8 grams with equal quantity of pepper soaked in water. Seed paste applied externally for skin conditions.

medicinal_uses: 1. Consuming the bark decoction helps destroy intestinal worms. 2. Applying a paste of fruits with rock salt and lemon juice on skin patches (vitiligo) helps heal them. 3. Consuming a decoction of leaf or bark helps treat diarrhea and dysentery.
dosage_preparation: Veterinary use: Take 60 grams of dried leaves from one branch, mix with a quarter seru of ragi and half seru of water, soak overnight, and administer to cattle. This treatment helps with blood-related and similar conditions.

medicinal_uses: 1. Consuming the buds and leaves helps treat gonorrhea and urinary problems. 2. A decoction of bark or its powder helps reduce rheumatic (vata) conditions and purifies blood. 3. Grinding the leaves and applying on the body helps eliminate body odor. 4. Consuming the bark decoction acts as a virilizer and enhances male vitality.
dosage_preparation: Bark decoction or leaf decoction taken orally; leaf paste applied externally for body odor.

medicinal_uses: 1. Drinking a decoction of the creeper helps cure hysteria and epileptic conditions. 2. Grinding the leaves and applying as a poultice on the head helps relieve headaches and also reduces hair fall. 3. Consuming the fruits helps act as a laxative similar to other remedies.
dosage_preparation: Creeper decoction taken internally; leaf paste applied to the scalp.

medicinal_uses: 1. Grinding the leaves, mixing with buttermilk and consuming helps relieve cough; mixing with sesame oil and consuming relieves breathing difficulties. 2. Applying a paste of the plant juice with a little turmeric helps heal bedsores; if insects are present in the wound, the paste also helps eliminate them. 3. Mixing the plant juice with coconut oil and applying on the body, especially for treating scabies, and also using green turmeric paste applied on the skin, helps cure skin infections. 4. Mixing leaf juice with sesame oil and applying on areas of the body affected by rheumatic pain, particularly on joints at night, helps reduce inflammation. 5. A decoction of the leaf juice mixed into medicated oil used in small doses with other preparations helps treat certain types of asthma and respiratory diseases.
dosage_preparation: Leaf juice mixed with sesame oil or buttermilk for internal use; paste applied externally for bedsores and scabies.

medicinal_uses: 1. Drinking a decoction of the bark helps reduce body heat caused by pitta (bile) imbalance. 2. Boiling the leaves in milk and adding to other preparations like leafy vegetables helps provide nourishment; applying the leaf paste externally on skin removes blemishes. 3. Grinding the leaves and applying as a poultice helps reduce swelling; using the same paste as a plaster provides relief from joint inflammation and stiffness. 4. Consuming the leaves helps address urinary tract problems, promotes diuresis, and treats conditions related to the reproductive system; it also helps prevent stone-related and respiratory conditions.
dosage_preparation: Bark decoction taken orally; leaves consumed as vegetable or applied externally as paste. Note: White basale (B. alba L.) is also used similarly.

medicinal_uses: 1. Grinding the tuber into powder (5 grams) and consuming with water or honey 3-4 times a day helps reduce fever and body heat. 2. Mixing tuber powder (about 5 grams) with jaggery and consuming after meals acts as a digestive tonic. 3. The tuber powder when consumed helps treat indigestion and strengthens digestion. 4. Grinding the tuber and consuming with appropriate liquid helps treat cough and related respiratory conditions. 5. The tuber decoction helps cure diarrhea and dysentery. 6. The tuber powder helps treat worm infestations. 7. Consuming tuber juice or decoction helps treat skin rashes and skin diseases. 8. A mixture of tuber powder with honey helps heal mouth ulcers. 9. Tuber powder mixed with turmeric, ginger, pepper, and castor oil is used to treat joint pain and rheumatoid arthritis.
dosage_preparation: Tuber powder: approximately 5 grams with water or honey, 3-4 times daily for fever. Tuber decoction for digestive and respiratory conditions.

latin_name: Hygrophila auriculata (K.Schum.) Heine (= Hygrophila longifolia Nees = Asteracantha longifolia (L.) Nees)
medicinal_uses: 1. Grinding the whole plant and mixing with clarified butter, then consuming with cow's urine in small quantities, mixed with milk, helps treat urinary disorders and also prevents gonorrhea-like conditions. 2. Drinking a decoction of the plant or consuming it as a vegetable helps treat gout. 3. Consuming seeds, black gram, and other ingredients together provides nourishment and helps treat urinary and reproductive disorders. 4. Taking approximately 3-6 grams of seed powder helps increase sperm count (the seeds contain 4 stamens, one of which is larger). 5. Grinding the plant extract and consuming 1-3 grams daily, along with other supplements, helps treat urinary infections and related conditions. 6. Taking a quarter spoon of seed powder with appropriate liquid for 2-3 days helps reduce swelling and inflammation.
dosage_preparation: Seed powder: 3-6 grams; Plant extract: 1-3 grams daily. Seeds with milk or water for reproductive health.

medicinal_uses: 1. Applying fresh bark paste on the mouth and lips of individuals with mouth sores helps them heal quickly. 2. Grinding the root of the plant, mixing with sesame oil, and preparing a medicated oil to apply on the ears helps reduce ear pain and inflammation.
dosage_preparation: Bark paste applied directly on mouth sores; root ground with sesame oil for ear drops.

medicinal_uses: 1. Consuming a decoction of bark helps treat cold, cough, and associated symptoms. 2. Consuming a decoction of bark also helps reduce body pain, fever, and treats cold and related conditions. 3. Applying bark paste on wounds and injuries helps in healing. 4. Consuming bark decoction over a period helps improve digestion (it is noted that those who use it should avoid certain foods).
dosage_preparation: Bark decoction for oral consumption; bark paste for external wound application. Veterinary use: grinding bark of the tree and applying on cattle wounds helps healing.

medicinal_uses: 1. Garike grass juice (4-6 spoons) mixed with sugar, along with curd, is taken daily on an empty stomach in the morning for conditions like piles; this treatment helps stop bleeding in piles and related issues. 2. About 2 handfuls of fresh garike grass are soaked overnight and the strained liquid is mixed with a quarter glass of tender coconut water; consuming this daily along with a restricted diet helps treat various conditions. The treatment duration is approximately 14 days. 3. Garike grass juice applied on the forehead and temples helps relieve headaches caused by sun exposure. 4. [continued on next page] 5. Making juice from the whole plant and consuming 7-14 mL daily for several days helps cure urinary disorders. 6. Consuming garike grass juice mixed with cumin and turmeric powder helps address vomiting, excessive thirst, lethargy, insomnia, and dream-related conditions.
dosage_preparation: Fresh juice: 4-6 teaspoons with sugar on empty stomach. Whole plant juice: 7-14 mL daily for urinary disorders. Duration: typically 14 days for hemorrhoid treatment.

latin_name: Eclipta alba (L.) Hassk. (= E. prostrata (L.) L.)
medicinal_uses: 1. Applying fresh plant juice on the nose (a few drops) helps cure snakebite poison. 2. Taking equal parts of leaf and peppercorn, grinding well, rolling into small pills, and consuming with buttermilk helps treat indigestion and related conditions. 3. Drinking fresh plant juice helps treat diarrhea and fever. 4. In the early morning, grinding the fresh plant into a paste and applying on bald patches on the scalp every alternate day for about 1-2 months helps promote hair growth. 5. Taking one spoonful of plant juice (from the wild variety) and consuming daily in the morning helps treat epilepsy; this treatment should continue for 1-2 months. 6. Mixing one part of the plant juice with a quarter part of lemon juice and consuming with daily food items like curd, honey, etc., helps treat cough, phlegm, anemia, skin diseases, and wasting conditions. 7. Grinding the plant and applying the paste on the right ear area helps treat left-sided ear pain, and vice versa, to provide counter-relief for ear infections. 8. Grinding the fresh plant with sesame oil and applying on the head daily before bathing, then washing after some time, helps treat vitiligo and leukoderma. 9. Mixing plant juice with the juice of wild drum plant, juice of nelli fruit, oil from haritaki, amla, and sesame oil to make a medicated oil helps treat various conditions related to scalp and skin. 10. Consuming a mixture of plant juice with other herbal combinations provides a cooling effect and promotes overall health.
dosage_preparation: Fresh juice: 1 spoonful daily for epilepsy (1-2 months). Paste applied to scalp for hair growth every alternate day. Mixed preparation with lemon juice for anemia and skin diseases. Veterinary use: fresh plant powder mixed with forest tree bark, cinnamon, cloves, and water given to cattle.

medicinal_uses: 1. Grinding the roots and leaves into a paste, adding milk, and applying on the scalp and hair helps promote hair growth and prevent greying; it can also be used to darken grey hair. 2. Consuming the root decoction with milk and fruit helps treat conditions related to spermatorrhea. 3. Placing the root in the mouth and chewing while allowing the juice to be swallowed helps eliminate intestinal worms when combined with regular use. 4. Consuming leaf juice helps purify blood, treats sore throat, and reduces cough and body aches. 5. Applying leaf juice on affected areas helps prevent skin darkening and pigmentation. 6. Mixing leaves with sesame oil and applying helps treat joint pain and rheumatic conditions. 7. Grinding the seeds as a paste and applying on areas bitten by dogs helps as a first-aid treatment. 8. Consuming leaf juice helps promote urination and treats urinary retention. 9. Grinding the seeds and making paste, applying on skin affected by skin worms/ringworm and repeating this treatment every 3 days helps cure the condition. 10. Grinding the seeds with fragrant substances like gandha and applying on skin helps treat skin discoloration and darkening until hair returns to normal color. 11. Using the seed and root extract with a mixture of camphor on paintings and cloths helps preserve them. 12. Taking 1-3 gunjas of seed decoction powder, heating, and consuming with milk helps enhance male virility and strength. 13. Soaking seeds in water, grinding, and applying slightly warm on boils helps them mature and drain quickly, reducing pain. 14. In small appropriate quantities, consuming seed bark decoction helps expel intestinal parasites.
dosage_preparation: Root decoction with milk for spermatorrhea. Seed paste applied externally for skin conditions. 1-3 gunjas of seed powder with warm milk for virility. CAUTION: Seeds contain toxic substances (abrin) and must be used with extreme care. Veterinary use: Gulaganja root decoction applied to reduce inflammation. Also: raw gulaganja root 3 tola, dried ginger 3 tola, pepper 3 tola, each ground to powder, mixed, and given in 1 seru of water for 5-6 days to treat cattle diseases.

latin_name: Lawsonia inermis L. (= L. alba Lam.)
medicinal_uses: 1. Consuming fresh henna leaf juice (7-14 mL) mixed with water (50-100 mL) and a little honey, taken 3 times daily, helps treat jaundice and liver-related disorders. 2. Applying henna (madarangi) leaf paste (2 spoonfuls) mixed with a glass of buttermilk consumed 1-2 times daily helps treat stomach ulcers and related conditions. 3. Making a decoction of henna leaves, mixing with milk, sugar, and honey, and consuming for 24 days helps strengthen the body and purify blood. 4. Grinding henna leaves with onion, peppercorn, and lemon juice, applying as a paste helps prevent hair fall and reduces dandruff. 5. Grinding henna leaves, applying on the fingers and nails, helps remove fungal infections and strengthens nails. 6.
